We're just able to kind of feel that, like the pain he was going through and you're like, God, he's be at instant like rapport with him as a character. And if Magneto would have came and been a really bad story to it, he came like, hey guys, so why was the kid at the Nazi concentration camp and they killed my mom. It sucked. I was really mad. So I destroyed the whole thing. And then you look at you guys and you're like, oh, I guess you had to be there. You missed it. Right? Like that's what most of us do when we tell our stories. So if you want to tell stories where you feel like that, it comes down to understanding, like when you're telling the story, explaining the surroundings and how you're feeling. If you're really like a good book where the author will spend 20 or 30 pages and I'm just explaining the room and all this stuff is happening and then the feelings before they ever have this two minute actual interaction with somebody. So when the time that interaction happens, like, dang, you feel the whole thing. So we tell our stories. We got to do the same thing. We have to get good at helping people to experience that. So instead of just saying that, I used to say, if I was to tell a story about one of my stories was when I first started hiring employees and I had 13 of my friends I hired and we were totally losing money. And it was Christmas. It was like two weeks before Christmas and I was out hanging up Christmas lights and I really thinned code on. I remember like, I remember putting it on, but like the wind kept blowing and it was like cutting through my, I gotta even fill the code. It was like cutting through me and I had this little staple gun because I didn't know how to hang up Christmas lights. It was my first year and I got this little wobbly ladder. I remember like, I didn't have gloves on you because I wanted to use the staple gun and I was like holding it. It was freezing cold. My hands were like so cold and I touched the thing, but I had to go up. So I was kind of like hiding and I was climbing up this thing. I got the staple gun out and I'm listening. I'm stapling these Christmas lights and as I'm doing it, I'm listening on my headphones to a marketing conference because at that time my entire company was about to go bankrupt. It was two weeks before Christmas and I knew that the next morning I was gonna have to call at the time as my friends and my family members and basically I had to let them go two weeks before Christmas because not only did I have money to cover their paychecks for the last two weeks, I had no money for the next two weeks. As I thought about firing my friends and two of my brothers and potentially letting go of other people that I had so much to suffer for, I was sitting there hanging this up and this feeling in my gut started coming and I felt like a heart attack, but it wasn't here. It was down lower and it was just like the stabbing pain and I almost couldn't lift my hands up to staple because I kept feeling that. I remember feeling like this pain, but at the same time I was freezing cold, but my hands were sweating and I couldn't understand it because it was like, why is it so cold? Why did I sweat coming from my hands? So much like it barely hold on this thing. I think like, how in the world am I gonna call these guys tomorrow and let them know that there's no Christmas bonuses, not even Christmas. Like, sorry, you're not gonna have money to pay for your wife's and your kids. Like, what a let down. I remember feeling like the weight of the world on my shoulders were like, I just didn't want to even go back inside. I spent hours out there hanging lights, listening to this marketing thing, hoping for some ray of hope of something I could do that would give us the freedom so we could continue business. Okay, there's a whole story after that, but I'm gonna tell it that way. Do you feel a little bit more? How many of you guys have like your palms are sweating right now? And so most of us, you gotta come back and explain, not just like, here's the thing that happened, but it's explaining how you felt. And if you explain how you were feeling as you're talking about as a epiphany, people will feel the same thing you were feeling. They'll feel that pressure. And if you had pressure come off, like the release, like you're explaining those types of things, people will experience that with you. And then when you share the story, then you're not gonna tell them, this is the epiphany I had, you tell the story. And if you do it right, they have the epiphany. Like that's the key. I'm not telling them my epiphany. I'm telling them the story. And then in that process, they're gonna be like, wow, and they're gonna have the epiphany. Like that's the goal. But I gotta get them in the same state that I was in as I'm sharing this with them, so they're gonna miss it. Okay. And that's why the storytelling stuff is so cool when you do it right. And it's all coming back into that, like helping them to feel what you felt so they can experience what you experienced when you're sharing these things. Okay. Any questions about that at all? So exciting. It's always hard at first to do. So I always try to stop and think about the things I see around me, think about how I'm feeling, think about things that they tend to be like heart, stomach, hands, head, pressure. Like those are the things that people can, like you talk about, I feel like a 600 pound weight on my back. Like I couldn't keep my spine straight, and though there's nobody actually on me. Like people, like you'll see them all slouching because like they're all feeling that same pressure. It's pretty cool. Now a couple examples of this. One was at last phone hacking live with Marcus Lamona speak. I've shared this with some of you guys before, but I had like a 30 minute window before he spoke to kind of get to know him and talk with him and we put that in the contract so I want to hang out with him. And so I had the same thing with Tony coming up. So we, so I'm in this room with him and there's like, Todd was there and a couple of us were sitting around and first thing he does he asks, he's like, okay, so walk by the bedroom. I thought you guys website platform, like what we are. He's like, well, why is everybody so excited out there? I was like, oh, well, and all of a sudden I start spewing out like this technical babble and like, oh, we built people's sales funnels and he's like, all right, but why is everyone so excited out there? Like they're jumping around like a rock concert. I'm like, okay. And then like luckily I stopped myself in here because I do the same thing. I was like, why are we all excited? I came back here when I came back as I started telling them a story. I told them a story about why this is such a cool thing. Why funnels are so exciting. I gave them my epiphany and he's sitting there listening. He's like, okay, he's like, well, how does this work for this business? And he's like, how would it work for sweet peeps? How does it work for camping world? And he asked me a couple of different businesses. I told him two or three and he's like, huh, every funnel or every business needs a funnel. I was like, yes, but he had the epiphany. I didn't like him. Marcus, does it deal every business needs a funnel? You should have me on the show. And he said, huh, every business needs a funnel. Do you want to be on the show? Okay. That's the difference. So I don't tell them I'm leading them to this thing. I need them to have the epiphany, not me telling them the epiphany. If I just give it to them, they're going to miss it. Okay. And that company Ripplin, I'm going to find it real quick. One of the guys I was working with on the concept, when we rolled that out, he had written a script initially. He was like, here's the script. I hope I can find it. And it was just like, it was a horrible script. He was basically giving people epiphany. Oh, this is the end. It was such like a network marketing pitch. And I remember email that dude that night. I put it in the books. I thought it was important. I said, Hey man, what I found is that when you're just selling something, it's not as strong. It doesn't create the emotion you need to really cause action. If you want people to adopt a new concept and really get their buy-in, you have to lead them to the answer, but you can't give it to them. They have to come up with the idea themselves. You plant the idea in their minds with a story. And if they come up with the answer, then they sold themselves. The buying decision becomes theirs, not yours. When that happens, you don't sell them anything. That's the goal I'm trying to do. We're creating story so that they go on this journey with me and all of a sudden they're like, Dang, I need that thing. And you're like, Yes, you do. And then it's off. That point four is all them. Okay. So that's this whole story stuff. Like, so exciting, but that's the power behind this, you guys. Okay. Now a couple of like really quick things we put in the book that I think are more important I want to share. One thing is, so you asked us now before, like there's technical things, a lot of times we have to share. And so for me, what I do, every time I'm explaining anything, and you probably noticed it through that last day and a half, I'll go into something and I'll be telling the story. And also I hit something I have to share because it's part of the thing, right? So like when I did the pitch for, for prove it, I say like, da da da da da da da da da key tones and soon as I hit that word key tones, I know that everyone's like, boom, technical babble and everyone like instantly is just like, the zone out. So every time I'm telling a story, I hit a technical thing. I stop right there. And I do what I call kind of like bridge. I say, it's kind of like a million motivational speakers running through your body, giving you insane amounts of energy. And they're like, Oh yeah, like that's pretty, like that's how I do it. So every time I hit any kind of concept that I think people are going to be stuck on, I stop and I'm like, it's kind of like, boom, and that's like my bridge. Every time I hit something. So I'm telling the story, there's going to be technical things in this journey. Every time I hit that, I stop and I do kind of like bridge. And it's just really quickly like new concept, what do they already understand? The new concept, what is it something that, that it's kind of like that they can already understand? And as soon as you bridge that, they're like, Oh, this makes perfect sense. Thank you so much. And that's the key. Like they're just, they're super fast. Every time you have a technical babble has to slip out for them to understand a piece. The second slips out, I switch, boom, it's kind of like this. And then, and then they understand. Kind of like bridges are the coolest. All right, let's see. Okay. The next section is long. I want to take a break before we do that. So let's do this. Okay, let's do this. So, all right. Okay. So we're going to transition to right now is the heroes two journeys. So a few years ago, Dagen Smith and I did an event called the invisible funnel together is in DC. And I think you guys all have access to it in the inner circle members area. It's an older one, but it was amazing. We had this guy come out and speak. His name is Michael Hague. You were there. Yes. I think your only person was there. Todd was there. Yes. Two people were there. It was good. So Michael Hague, we, we, we pay this dude from Hollywood to come and speak to us. It's Michael Hague and he's got a course. I told you guys about some of you guys about this. It's called the heroes two journeys. And it was him and this guy, Chris Vogue, go through this concept called the heroes two journeys. And he came and spoke at our event and walked us through this concept. And then for the last like 10 years, it's been, or five years, it's been like percolating through my brain and trying to figure out how does what he shared like relate to what we do. And it took me a long time to like bridge that gap, but it's, it's like the coolest thing in the world. So the heroes two journeys based on like 98% of all Hollywood films fit into this structure. And what Michael Hague was telling us, basically like, you look at any movie, he's like, from a screen, from a screenwriter standpoint, you know, then every single movie and like page X of this script, the hero always does this on page, this in the script, they always do this on pay. And it's like the same. And you can plug any movie, almost any movie into, into this process, which is fascinating. He was like, this is storytelling at its roots. Like this is every movie this ever become big, all follows this and the majority of all movies, all stories do. And so he explained it all. I was like, God, that's so cool. But you know, we're not writing Hollywood movies. Like how do we actually make that something tangible we can do? And so that's the next section. I'll be talking about that. But first of all, you guys understand this piece of it. Because when you understand this piece of it, then it makes your actual storytelling so much cooler and so much easier. Okay. All right. So I'm going to go through this piece by piece by piece. All right. So the first thing that he talked about is, and here's two journeys that basically every good story has three core things. There's a character. The character has desires. And then on his way to get those desires, there's conflict. That's it. Okay. That's all like in the most basic primal state that is what story is. Three things. Character, desire, conflict. Okay. And so, for example, the example he shared when he spoke at our event was Little Red Writing. He said, Little Red, Little Red Riding Hood, actually I think it's Little Red Riding Hood, has a desire to take a basket of cookies for grandma. But on the journey there, there's conflict. There's a big bad wolf who wants to eat her. That's the story. So that's the plot statement, right? I'm going to go back. So all the things happened. And one thing he talked about, he said that most people think that the emotion in a story is caused by the desire. Because for you, for the hero, like your desire is like, I want that thing. I want to win this thing, right? See this desire you're going for? And that's what causes emotion for you. But nobody cares about the thing the hero is trying to get. Like what causes the emotion is the conflict in the middle. What makes the story actually interesting is the desire, is the conflict. So without conflict, it's not a good story. So there's always a character, there's a desire, and there's conflict. So again, here's the Red Riding Hood plot statement. So I want you guys to think back about your origin story, how you had your first initial epiphany about what it is that you do right now. And I want you to think of it in these three things. There's a character, which is probably you. There's the desire you had, which is to make money, lose weight, invest in whatever your thing is. What's the desire? And then on your journey to get that thing, what was the conflict that you ran into? Okay, so all you guys are now working on is you're going to write a plot statement. It's basically this. So if mine was, my name is Russell Brunson. I had a desire to be able to make enough money my wife could quit her job. And the conflict I met along the way is it turns out it's really hard to build websites. And I had all these issues that came along, right? Super simple. So everyone, let's take a minute and write a plot statement for your origin story. The thing that got you to where right now, character, desire and conflict. I had a burning desire to build a business and network marketing. I just felt compelled. It's something I was just aching to do inside, but I didn't know how to be a mom and to be a successful business owner. And what I wanted to bless my family's life started taking me away from them. Cool. That's a good conflict. Awesome. Thank you. Two more shares. Whoa. That was close. That was close. The character is my mom, Dr. Jennifer Daniels. I think her desire was to help people or cure people. And her conflict was just the whole medical industrial complex. And she has to know her real story. It's insane. All right. Nick. Guys transitioning out of the military wants to be successful like he was in the service. Little did he know there were nine transition traps waiting to gobble him up. Nice. Cool. All right. So that's the first thing I've got to figure out in any story is the plot line. Is that. So like the nice thing about stories, you can tell a story in like 10 seconds or like 10 hours, like just by filling in the details of all this kind of stuff. So as you come back through, so with the character, there are a couple of things that Michael Hague taught me that it's like for every character in a movie, there are basically there's five things that you can do to build rapport with the audience. The character needs to have at least two of these five. And typically the first 10% of every single movie, the backstory, the origin story is all about like getting you to actually care about the character. If you don't care about the character, then this whole journey just becomes boring and people don't want to, they don't care. So the first time to the movie is all about building rapport with the hero. So they do it through, again, five things and they try to get at least two of these five things. So either the person needs to be a victim, which makes you care about them. They need to have like something in jeopardy, life in jeopardy, the job in jeopardy, something that's going to be falling apart. They got to be likable, they got to be funny, or they got to be powerful. Because you're thinking that there's two of those things that are going to be introduced to your audience for them to have rapport with you enough to actually care about your story. If there's not, they're like, I remember watching, it's one of the end of the world movies. I remember watching it and like the end, the character died and I was like, I don't even care. Like I'm glad he died. He was kind of annoying. I remember the whole movie was ruined for me because there was no like, I didn't care. So the big thing is you have to care about the character or else the rest of the story doesn't even matter. So it's all about building rapport. And those are, again he said in movies that when they try to, they try to get two of the five. The second thing is then there's the desire where they're trying to get. He says that typically the desire is one of four things. They're trying to win something when a race, when a girl, when whatever they're trying to win something, or they're trying to retrieve something, trying to go somewhere and get something and bring it back, or they're trying to escape from some bad guys, or they're trying to stop something. Thinking about most movies, you think about any movie you can plug through this, Disney movies, real movies, superhero movies. Like they're always, the desire is one of those four things typically. So it's like, what's the desire of the character? I want to win this thing. I want to stop whatever that thing might be. Okay, so for your story it's the same thing like when you went on this journey, for your origin story, like which one of those is it for you? You're trying to retrieve something, trying to escape from some life you hated, trying to stop something bad from happening. Okay, so now you come back here to this slide. So this is kind of showing, I'm going to keep building on this slide each time. So now we've got the three core things in the plot line. Here's the attractive character, and here's how we build our port. Like I said, the first 10% of every single movie, so he was saying like on page, whatever it was, 22 in the screenplay is always the first 10% and that's when the hero leaves. Like physically leaves where they're at. Frodo leaves the Shire, right? Lightning McQueen leaves the racetrack. Bell leaves her house. It's always like the character is leaving. So the initial they're there in this environment where you're understanding the character, you're building rapport with them, and then the character actually physically leaves. There's a lot of my videos, if you're noticed, so like I'm not just standing on my desk, I'm physically leaving like, hey, I'm getting in my car, come on guys, let's go. And I jump and I'm leaving, so I'm trying to take somebody with me on a journey. Like I'm physically leaving. Frank Kern's great at this too. He always watches the videos. He's never there in front of the desk. He's like, hey guys, how's it going? Come on over here. I'm leaving and bringing people on a journey with them. I was talking to Dan during the break, there's a video I want to make here where like someone opens the door and they're coming to ClickFunnels and I welcome them to ClickFunnels and I take them and we walk to a different place. As soon as they're doing that, they're following you on this journey. So I don't mind doing it like as I'm speaking, but like a lot of times physically in the video or however you're using it, I'm leaving on this journey, right? Now what's happening is that, again this is called the hero's two journeys. The first journey is what we call the journey of achievement. This is what the hero's setting out to. They want to do this thing, right? And that's what they're always thinking about. Like I got to go win this race and I find out like this thing's happening. So this is like what's very known to the audience. The audience knows like Frodo's leaving because he's going to take the ring and he's going to leave the shy. He's coming over here. He's got to drop it into the volcano and destroy the ring. Like that's the journey of achievement. I got to achieve this end goal to get my desires, right? And then the second journey down here is called the journey of transformation. This is the journey that we're not aware of by watching the outside. This is what's happening where the character actually is transforming as a person. Okay, so the two journeys, journey of achievement and the journey of transformation. Okay? Now if you come back here, this is the journey of transformation, the second journey. What's happening is during the back story of the character, you're learning about their identity. Like this is who I am. This is who I believe that I am. Like I believe these things are important to me. All of this is happening, right? Think about any movie. Like these are the things that are important to me. And then as they leave and they go on this journey, all this conflict starts happening and all their beliefs, like the things that they hold true themselves, like the identity they have, they start realizing that it's not good or it's wrong or something. And they go through this journey and all these things start falling apart. Eventually at the end of it, the journey of transformation happens and they become a new person. They have a new belief system because of this journey, right? So if you look at this from the hero's two journeys. This journey right here, journey of achievement, down here is the journey of transformation where you're leaving your own identity. So I'm going to kind of tell you guys a story from a movie and I'm going to plug it into this framework so you can kind of see the whole thing. I'm going to share it with the movie from Cars. Have you guys seen Cars like 50 times? So good. So Lightning McQueen is the character. He has a desire to win the piston cup. He runs into conflict when he gets stuck in radiator springs. Boom, there's a storyline. So the first 10% of the movie, we are at a certain location where the character feels safe, feels very, very good, things are happening and we're introduced to him, right? So you start going through and things are happening. We see he's powerful, right? He's kind of funny. He's doing things like that, right? And we're building this rapport with him. You're seeing him racing. The first rookie ever to win the piston cup is all exciting. You have this whole backstory. And then he loses it or they tie, right? They three-way tie and it ends. And so because he has to go on this journey, if he wants to achieve his thing, he wants to win the piston cup, I got to go on this journey. I have to go retrieve the piston cup. I have to go win it. I have to leave, right? So he gets in the car and we have this really cool glimpse of Lightning McQueen that makes us actually care about him. Because the first is like this cool, powerful guy, super awesome. Then he gets in the car and then his agent gets on the phone and he starts talking to the agent. And the agent basically lets in this little glimpse so we know that Lightning McQueen is actually super insecure and nobody really likes him. And that's why he's so powerful but he has no friends. He's like, hey, I got tickets for your friends. You don't want me to give them to you. He's like, oh, well, you're going to be the right one. I can't actually make it but I'll be watching your band. And we realize, wow, he's actually vulnerable. We start seeing the character flaws in Lightning McQueen. Like, wow, this thing happens, right? So then 10% of the movie, now we actually care about this character. We've got some relationship with him. And then he leaves. He's got to go on this journey of achievement. He's leaving and all of a sudden conflict happens, right? And he gets stuck in the radiator springs. He's locked up and he's going through all these things and he's trying to win. He's trying to get to the thing. He's got to get to your win Piston Cup over and over and over again. And all this conflict's happening thing after thing after thing and all this conflict's happening and throughout the entire movie, you can see all this conflict happening. Every time he tries to lead, he gets caught. This thing happens. This thing happens. And at the end of it, finally, he gets to go to the race. He shows up the Piston Cup. He goes out there and he's racing and he's kind of miserable and he's not having them. Like, he's doing well but he's not really that happy. And then Doc and all those guys come and they become the sideline, right? He goes out there and he's doing this whole race and he's getting a point where he actually has a shot. A shot to actually win this thing that he's been trying to get his whole entire life. And there's a point like he crashes and there's no way he can actually win. He comes back and he goes back through it again and finally the point is about to win. He's racing and he comes down to the finish line and he's just screaming through. He's beating everybody. He comes to the finish line, looks up to the monitor and he notices something just happened. Chick Hicks, who's like the jerk of the whole thing, he hits the king who he's racing in. He hits the king, the king's car flips out of control, gets completely destroyed, smashed up into little pieces. He looks up to the monitor and he sees the king, smashed on the side and realizes he's not going to finish this race. And all of a sudden his achievement becomes in jeopardy. The cool thing about the hero's two journeys, sometimes the hero achieves their thing, sometimes they don't. What's interesting, we don't really care if they achieve this. What's more interesting is the transformation that happens to the character down here. So what happens? Lighting Queen sees that, he slams on his brakes, it comes all the way to finish line, stops and Chick Hicks flies past him, wins the race. Lighting Queen backs up, comes over the king, gets behind him and starts pushing him to the finish line. And the king says, dude, what are you doing? He's like, you just lost yourself to Piston Cup. And then Lighting Queen says, oh, you know, an old friend of mine once told me, you know, it's just an empty cup. It's not that big a deal. And he pushes the king across and we have a chance to see that not only he misses his achievement, but he transformed as a person. That's the real journey. That's the journey we actually cared about was the journey of transformation. Isn't that awesome? I have wheels. Yeah. I was going through this thing a little while ago and I mapped it out and I was trying to figure out movies and I thought about that one and I was just like, how cool is that? Most of us, we're always trying to achieve this thing, achieve this thing, achieve this thing. We think that's what people actually care about, but it's not. We didn't care if Lighting Queen hit it or not. We cared about that. Him taking this insecure person with no friends and through the story, he came something different, became someone more. That's what we care about. That's why we love that movie. That's why I watch like 30 times with my kids because of that. That's the cool thing. It's the second journey. So that's kind of the framework of most movies. So I want you guys, I want you guys to think back through one of the last movies you saw, whichever one you remember the best. He is living in the streets of India as a poor Indian child. And him and his brother, he always wants to help his brother out, his older brother. And so he begs his older brother to take him to go, I guess, collect money. You're kind of understanding that he's going to go handle or something like that. So he's like, okay, you can come with me, but you have to stay right here on this train bench. So they ride the train together. They ride the train together to get to the next station. And he's like, you have to stay right here. Don't move. Well, he moves, gets on a train, goes like 2,000 miles away from home. He's five years old, wanders the streets trying to figure out what can I do as a child, right? He doesn't know anything. And it's showing this like, you're like really emotionally engaging in this suffering of this child. And he ends up getting picked up by like six traffickers. And he like gets away from them. And then this guy like finds him and he takes him to an orphanage and he gets adopted by parents from Australia. So then he goes even further from home in Australia. And he grows and then it flashes back to show him or flashes forward to show him as an adult. And he keeps having these flashbacks of being a little child at home. And he's like, I need to get home. I need to get home. Who are my parents? Who's my family? I don't know who they are. But if I can just find, if I can just find that station, that train station, I think I could get back home. And so he maps out the whole, all the train stations in India, which were a lot. And finally uses like the internet to find like the tower that he can vaguely remember goes back and walks his way back into the village and finds his mom. Wow. I want to watch the movie now. What's it called? Lion. Lion? That was awesome. I got chills just listening to the movie. Spoilers. All right. You want to watch it? Cool. All right. Okay. So let's come back. So my next question that after like, I was like a five year thing for me because I learned all stuff from him. I'm like, that's awesome. But like, what's the structure for me to like tell this in a room where I've got maybe two minutes or I've got 10 minutes or I've got an hour. Like, how do I do that? And I've tried over the years, like figure out different different processes. In fact, even in the inner circle, I think I've shared two or three different versions of what of the new script. And this is the one like a week and a half ago when I was writing it three in the morning, all of us like, oh my gosh, like it encompasses every. It took like five or six of my stories and I ran through this. I was like, this is like the first time where I was just like freaking out. So I don't think any of you guys have actually seen this image yet. But this is the most up to date version, the one that I am like super excited for of the actual Pifny Bridge script. So this is the magic. And this is going to be the template that all my stories run through now. And it's just, it makes this whole concept simple. I want to share the hero's journey. So you guys see from a higher, like here's how the whole thing works. How do we do it for like our intents and purposes? So these are kind of the different pieces of it. I'm going to walk through it all because if you get it all, like it's just really powerful. So first thing, first key is we always start with some kind of backstory, right? And part of it is like we talked about heroes to journey. Like you have to, people have to care who you are. And the problem is with you as an expert, they're seeing you up on this mountain. They're like, dang, like, look how awesome this person is. And if you're up there talking down to them, then they don't, there's no empathy. They don't care about you as much. So like for you to have, to have real empathy and impact on some of you, you have to get off of wherever you're at. The results you've already achieved and come back down over here to where they're at. And they like, Hey man, guess what? I was in this exact same spot. I remember what it was like and it sucked. There's some cool stuff over here. I can show you, but like I need to, I need to tell. And so you start your journey, your story there, right? And as I'm telling the story, I'm talking about the result I want, like the journey of, of achievement. I want to achieve this really, really cool thing. And we started going into the desires. Now with the desires, there's two kind of desires. There's the external desire that most of us are willing to share with people, right? Like, Oh, I want to lose weight or Oh, I want to make money or Oh, I want to, whatever that thing is, right? And most people only tell people around them, like, like we're all comfortable sharing our external desires. Okay. For all your business, like now let's use it really easy. The reality is like, that's not really what people want to do stuff. Like I don't want to lose weight because I want to lose weight. I want to lose weight because of some other, like there's an internal desire. That's the real thing that I'm trying to get and trying to accomplish. Right. And so usually to get to the internal, what I usually do, I'm asking someone if I'm trying to lose weight, why did you want to lose weight? Why did you want to end up keep asking why? Why? And usually it's like five or six or seven wise till they break down and they become vulnerable. And they're like, this is why. And that's like the interesting thing. And so what happens is when you're telling your story, you come to your back, when you talk to like, look, I wanted to make money. I wanted to lose weight. You tell your external desires. Like, cool. That's what I want to do too. And as soon as you're willing to like say, but the real reason I want to do it is because I want to lose weight. I want to lose weight. I want to lose weight. I want to lose weight. But the real reason I want to do it is this and you share this deep internal desire, the same thing that they almost everybody has. No one ever talks that level. And as soon as you do, it's like instant rapport with like, dang, I still want to. I've never told them that because I'm scared. They just told me and they were willing to tell me like, oh, like in all sense, like instant, instant connection. So for you, it's understanding in your story, like, here's the extra one I want, but then like digging deeper to figure out like why you actually wanted that. And most of you guys know it sometimes subconsciously because you don't, we hide it like we're taught to bury those kind of things. But if you ask enough why's, why's, why's, it'll come down to like, boom, I did this because I want to impress my dad or all my wife to accept me or all my kids look up to me or whatever like the, those core desires are. Like that's the real reason. Okay. And if you, as you look at heroes to journeys, like the first journey is the, is going after the external desire and the journey of transformation is, is actually the internal desires. It's pretty cool when you, when you realize that, right? So that's the first one. So I'm sharing those things initially cause I want, I want them to know that, that, that they, I feel what they feel. I've felt that before. And then that brings me now to this wall. So I have these desires. I have these things I want. Then I come to this wall and I'm stuck. And what I realized that morning a week and a half ago, guess what that wall is for everybody? It's the current opportunity you're in. It's the current opportunity they're in. So I'm in this wall. I'm in this opportunity. I've been trying to lose weight through this means, through this thing. And I can't do it anymore. I'm stuck. You were stuck and you show like this is, I hit this wall. I was trying to, to get that result. I was stuck in this vehicle. I hit this wall. I can't get forward because the vehicle sucks. Like I cannot, it's impossible for me to move forward cause the vehicle on me in. So for me to have success, I have to leave this vehicle. It's just scary because my whole life I was taught this is the way to do it. I have to get out of that vehicle and come over here to a new vehicle. And it's like, oh, scary. But I got to show them like I was willing to do that. Like my whole life I learned this is the way to do it. And as soon as I say, okay, I'm not getting my result. No matter how hard I try, I'm stuck in this wall. I'm stuck in this vehicle. I'm going to go on a journey cause I have to figure this out. I have to get that. That's my goal. Then I walk over here. Boom. And so then I started doing something started happening. Okay. And that's where I started telling the story where I have this epiphany. And the epiphany I have is the new opportunity. Like I had this epiphany. And I was like, wow, I need this thing. And that brings down like that presents this new opportunity. So this is the new vehicle I get into. I'm like, God, this epiphany. And I realized that was the thing. And I got into this new vehicle. Like, sweet, I'm in this vehicle. It's different. I don't know if it's going to work yet. I just got here. Now what do I do? Okay. Well, I have a plan. What I'm going to do is I'm going to try this, this and this. And so I go on this plan. This is my plan to try to get that result. I'm going through this plan. And somewhere through the plan there's this conflict that happens. As I was trying to execute this conflict and I struggled and I couldn't get through because of this thing that happened. But then at the end, two things happened. Number one, it's I achieved it or I didn't. I achieved it. So I got my external desires, which is cool. But the coolest thing is not that. The coolest thing, the kind of transformed. The internal desires I had back there, like that's what actually mattered. I'm showing them the resolution of the hero's two journeys. And that's the epiphany bridge script. Is that amazing? Oh, anyway, I got chills just going through. Like I'm so excited. And so that's kind of the process. And so I also broke it down into questions that make it super easy for us to be able to have it, right? So the first, if you look through this, there's a couple elements. There's eight elements. Backstory, desires, the wall, the epiphany, the plan, the conflict, achievement and transformation. Okay, and I spent that night. If you guys watched my Snapchat, that's what I was doing until 4.30 that morning was this. Like I was just like nuts and I'm like freaking out in Snapchat. I'm like, I can't believe this is happening. I'm freaking out. Like, ugh. So I wrote these questions in a way that gets me to actually do the right thing, right? So the backstory. So what's your backstory that gives us a vested interest in your journey? It's not what's your backstory, but why should I care about you? So that's the first question. And then you answer that. Well, boom, you tell the backstory that gives them a vested interest in your journey. Number two is what is the desire you wanted to accomplish? What were the external struggles you were dealing with and what are the internal struggles? I'm going to share those. Then we have the wall. What's the wall or the problem you hit within your current opportunity that started you on this new journey? It was physically impossible for me to grow and get what I wanted because of this opportunity I'm stuck in. Okay, then you had this epiphany that you experienced as you went on this journey. They introduced you to this new opportunity. Then you had a plan. What was the plan you created to achieve your desire? What was the conflict you experienced along the way? What was the result of what you achieved? And what was the transformation that you experienced? That's magic. Yeah. What do you guys think about that? Okay. It's been taking a while. I don't want to draft those. Good. You have three minutes. Just kidding. No, but I'm kind of serious. This is the plan between now and lunch. I want to push you guys. I'm going to push myself too. What I'm going to do and what we're all going to do is I want you guys to think, and this fits in any story, but specifically I want your origin story right now. We're going to be talking about all the sub stories after lunch, but right now the origin story, the theme that gave you the initial epiphany that got you into this vehicle to your right now. So what's going to happen is when we do the webinars tonight, the perfect webinar to start going through it, what happens is typically someone got on the webinar and the first thing I'm going to do is I'm going to introduce the big domino and I'm going to tell my Pifty Bridge story. But I'm just going to sit down here. And hopefully I ended up going off the wire fight yesterday, right? To make it work. All right, LT. Yeah, do we have, oh heck yeah. But John's going to have my story stool. I do now, I didn't before. Welcome to the story stool. Yeah, Dave's got, oh, I'm so excited. The stool story. Thank you very much. All right, cool. The story stool. Hey guys, the story, story stool. The story stool. Well, this story stool is autographed by a story teller. Just kidding. All right. Okay, I've never told a story live in all the details. I'm going to go through and hopefully won't mess up. I've got my notes. I'm sure I will. Okay, here we go. And we're going live in three, two, one. Hey everyone, this is Russell. Hope you guys are all doing awesome. So I'm here right now in the room of, I think, 48 or 49 amazing entrepreneurs. And you guys all see me again. And we've been talking about storytelling and how to tell your story, kind of the origin story of where you got started and what got you into what you're doing. And so I've been challenging all of them to go and do that. And I thought it would be kind of cool if I just told you guys my story. And some of you guys have heard pieces or parts of this, but probably not the whole thing. And so what I'm going to do is just kind of share with you guys a story about click funnels and why this for me became my new opportunity. The thing that I'm so excited about and why I have so much passion. Some people say, Russell, you've been talking about click funnels every day for the last two and a half years. I'm like, I know because I'm so excited every day. So some of the backstory, if you don't know, so I got started in this business, I guess it's almost like 13 or 14 years ago now. And when I did, I was a college guy. I was going to Boise State University, which is just down the road from right now. And I was a wrestler, which means I wrestled and I had to go to school. So I barely graduated. It was like barely. I think my acumen level is a 2.1, which means I got a whole bunch of C's and like 1B over five years. So it's kind of painful. But I graduated, which made my mom happy and was pretty cool. But during school, as I was wrestling, I started learning about this internet marketing stuff. And you have to remember, this is 12 years ago, it's before Facebook, it's before Instagram, all these kind of things. We had Google kind of. And in fact, I don't even remember how we were driving traffic. We didn't have website, anything like I was using front page. Some of the old timers remember front page. So I was creating these things in front page and it was pretty bad. So I was doing that. But I believed that it was possible. I saw people like some of my mentors were Armand Moran and Alex Mendoza and some of these guys who were making money online. I was like, God, these guys can do it. I'm pretty sure I can do it. So that was my goal I wanted to accomplish. Now, the other side of that I don't really share a lot of times was it wasn't just that I wanted to make money. That was a desire I had. But the bigger desires, I had just gotten married to my beautiful wife. We're still married now. It's been 15 years this year, which is amazing. And I married her. She was working full time to support me, her jobless wrestling husband. And I don't know about you, but I was a vision like when I got married, I'd be like the man. I would be like the supporter. And it was honestly pretty tough for me that she was supporting me. And I didn't have anything to contribute other than like I'm going to go to school and wrestle and hopefully. And it was hard for me. And I remember I kind of figured out a way to contribute. I don't want her doing everything. And so that was the real driving factor. If I can make $1,000 a month, that would match what she was making. We weren't making very much money at the time. And I was like, then I feel like I'm contributing as well. And so that was my goal. I need to figure out how to do this. And I tried all sorts of stuff. I was doing, I was trying eBay. I was trying Craigslist. I was doing stuff. And maybe I had like a little like a $20 a month, which was like a big deal for me. I was excited. But none of the things really hit big. And about the time I started learning about information marketing, in fact, I was at a post office mailing something I'd bought from eBay in this huge awkward box. I thought it was going to sell huge. And I didn't know if I could buck 50 or something. I lost tons of money. And then I did a post and it was a nightmare. And I'm sitting in the line at the post office next to this dude who had a big box full of CDs, like thousands of them. I'm like, dude, what are you selling? He's like, well, I have one CD I sell and I burn it. So these are orders for the month. He's like, no, these are my orders for this week. I'm like, I'm doing the math. How much are you selling for? He's like, $67. I was like, oh my gosh. And I had this like, I need to sell information. Like that is the future. So I started trying to figure out information. I started doing stuff. My very first product I ever created was a DVD on how to make potato guns. Some of you guys have heard this story before. So I started making potato guns. And it was working really, really good. But then what I was doing is I was going to Google. I was buying ads on Google. And we were making some money back and forth. But eventually, I probably had four or five months of success and then Google changed our algorithm and it got really, really hard. And the harder thing for me was during this time, it was me building things in front page. And I had to figure out how to like FTP pages and images and then how to go to PayPal and get the order button. It was super techy. And I'm not a techy person at all. But after Google changed our algorithm, we tried some stuff. And I remember one of my friends called me. It's Mike Philson. Some of you guys may know Mike. And he said, Russell, I figured out how to make this whole internet marketing game work again. I'm like, what do you mean? Is your potato gun thing still making money? I was like, no, I can't break even. I can't make a profit. He's like, well, I went back to all my sites and I added these things. And he called them OTOs. I was like, what's an OTO? He's like, it's an upsell and one time offer. I'm like, oh. So he's like, I had these upsells and people start buying the upsells. And suddenly, I'm making two or three times as much money from everything I sold. And I was like, dude, I can actually do that. And so I remember jumping online. I was Googling potato guns and trying to figure out what potato gun DVD. And I met this dude up in Northern Idaho who actually made potato guns and dropped shipped him. And I was like, dude, if I sell those, would you drop shipping for me? He was like, yeah. I loved to not have to sell these things. And so I partnered with him and I started selling DVDs and people started buying this upsell. And it was the first thing I made a funnel. And I was like, this is a funnel. And that was this new thing. I was so excited. And so I had this epiphany. Like, this is the future. Like, if I can make more funnels, like this funnel's going to make me like, I don't know, 20, 30 bucks a day. But what if I had two funnels, or three, or five, or 10? And so I started going a little bit crazy. And I created funnels in the Kuponny market, which by the way is a horrible market. People in that market do not like to spend money. So I don't do that one. But I did Kuponny. We did dating. We did weight loss. We did network marketing. I've done diabetic supplements. We've done, and that's like the ones on top of my head. Like, there's like, I show you guys, there's like a trail of thousands of offers. Some of them worked and a lot more that didn't work. But we were doing these things over and over. And my plan was like, if I had 10 of them that we should make 100 bucks a day, or 20, or 50, or like, what if I had it? So I was doing this. And at first it was working. But the problem I kept running into was like, front pages. For someone like me, it was hard. And I had to do these pages. And mine looked ugly. Other people's like, it looked awesome. So then I had a designer. He would design these things. So I'd make it in front pages ugly. I'd send to him. He'd design it. It looked cool. And I put it up. Then it was up. And then I couldn't do anything. I couldn't touch it again. I had one shot. And then I had to bug him. So then I hired a web guy to go and do edits for me. And then I was just using PayPal. And I couldn't do anything else. So then someone invented a one-click upsell. And so I was like, dude, I need that. So I had to hire a programmer to connect that to the next. And it kept getting more and more complicated to the point where, like, as soon as I wasn't able to do anything, I just looked at it and be like, change that. And then they tried to change it. And they didn't. I'm like, well, they move it over here. And then they put it in the wrong spot. And it would take days, sometimes weeks, going back and forth with the guys in Romania and India and everywhere trying to get them to just move an image and make it a little bit bigger. Things that were so common sense. But I couldn't actually do it. And it drove me nuts. And about that time, one of my friends and partners, in fact, he may not be in the room right now. Is Todd in here still? No. Oh, he's already in lunch. So he's already gone. But Todd was one of my partners that were doing these things with me. And he saw my frustration. He's like, what if we built something where, and the joke was that Russell could actually edit the website and actually could move things. He could quit bugging us all the time. And I was like, dude, that'd be awesome. He's like, OK, that's what we're going to create. And that day, we sat in front of a white board and we were showing him a map. And I was like, what would it look like? And how would it work? And so we mapped out this vision of what became click funnels. And he went home and actually built it. And then it was funny him. And then another part we brought in was Dylan Jones. They were working. He did the editor. And they would build part of it. And they're like, we need to make this so simple. Russell can do it. So they do it. And they're like, hey, Russell, test this. And I log in. And I'm like, how do I do it? He's like, we're not going to tell you. You've got to figure it out on your own. I'm like, OK. So I'd like do some stuff. And if I could figure it out, then I'm like, good, we got it. If I got stuck, I'm like, I don't know what to do. Then I'm like, hey, we didn't do it intuitively enough. So I went back and forth and back and forth for probably six or seven months. And finally, it was a point where I got it. I was like, if I can do this, then anyone can. And first, it was just a tool for me. That was really the initial goal of ClickFunnels. I think it was it taught me to go like, this way Russell will quit bugging us. And I don't have to design sites anymore or redo his headers. All this stuff that was happening. And then after I started using it, and I was having success, I was like, we have to make this a tool for everyone. It'll free all entrepreneurs. And it freed me. And so because of that, I was able to start creating funnels quickly. I went from having five or six guys and take us on average almost two to three months every single time to build a funnel, where I was actually able to do it by myself in an hour and hour and a half sometimes. Sometimes less, which was cool. And so the cool thing is I was able to achieve that and have success. We started rolling out funnels like crazy. Some of you guys probably remember seeing a lot of them. I still do a lot of them because it's so much fun to do. But the cooler thing for me was the other side. I was able to make money to support my wife and support my kids. Because I didn't no longer had to spend three months doing something, and my time to be with my kids is completely freed up. I have a chance to go home every day and play with my kids. I'm not stressing out trying to talk to programmers in India three in the morning and stressing all this stuff. It just works. And that was really the biggest thing for me. And it's been an amazing thing. In fact, now my kids are using click funnels. They're building stuff in click funnels. And for me, it's not just a product. It's a mission. I'm seeing people's lives change. I'm seeing other people who had messages, but they couldn't get them out because they couldn't move things around on this stupid page. We're now like the power is back in their hand. And that's what fires me up is being able to help entrepreneurs and other kind of things. So that's kind of my click funnel story. So if you guys haven't heard it before, I want to share that with you guys and to share why I'm so passionate about this. And for me, it's not just I talk about this all the time, but it's not a matter if you're going to become a click funnels member. It's when. Because it's the only way to do what we're talking about. The only alternative is to do what I just used to do 10 years ago and hire guys in Romania and India and the Philippines. I have a team of 30 people to be able to move an image. Or you can just use click funnels. And it gives you and me, the entrepreneurs, the power back, which is awesome. So I hope that helps you guys. And I hope you guys see why I'm so passionate about click funnels. And with that said, I'm going to check out. And if you like this, comment down below. If you have any friends who are like, what is that click funnely thing, share this to them.
